coredata.dc:title: 3D CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti photoelectrode with dominant {001} facets: Efficient visible-light-driven photoelectrocatalytic oxidation of organic pollutants and mechanism insight
coredata.dc:creator: Niu, Qiongyan
Gu, Xiaotong
Li, Lei
Zhang, Ya-nan
Zhao, Guohua
coredata.dc:description: A three dimensional (3D) carbon quantum dots (CQDs) decorated {001}TiO2/Ti photoelectrode was prepared by constructing the CQDs on the surface of {001} TiO2, which were grown in-situ on Ti-mesh structure, providing the nearly 100% exposed active {001} facets. The 3D CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti shows high PEC oxidation activity towards the MO and BPA under visible light radiation, while the relevant removal ratios are 94.8% for BPA in 1 h and 93.6% for MO in 2 h, respectively. The enhanced PEC activity of CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti is mainly attributed to the three improved factors (η abs, η sep and η inj), which is analyzed deeply. The existence of CQDs not only improve the η abs since its superior up-conversion performance, but also promote the higher η sep due to enhanced hole-electron pairs’ separation. The highly exposed {001} facets are favorable for oxidation reactions to provide the increased η inj.
coredata.dcterms:subject: 3D CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti
Carbon quantum dots
Up-conversion
PEC oxidation
Visible light irradiation

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: PEC performance of 3D CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti under visible light irradiation
PEC degradation and oxidation kinetic of BPA and MO

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The illustration of the PEC mechanism on CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti photoelectrode.

SEM images of (A) Ti mesh substrate and (B) {001}TiO2/Ti (inset: the high magnification SEM image); TEM images of (C) {001} TiO2, (D) CQDs, (E) and (F) {001} TiO2 before and after loading the CQDs (insets: the corresponding HRTEM images).
(A) UV–vis diffuse reflectance absorption spectra (insert: the transformed Kubelka–Munk function versus the photon energy), (B) The up-converted PL properties of CQDs.
(A) LSV curves, (B) I–t curves, (C) Nyquist plots, and (D) Mott-Schottky plots of the four photoelectrodes, respectively.

(A) and (C) The PEC degradation of BPA and MO over CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti and {001}TiO2/Ti under visible light irradiation ([BPA] = 1mg L−1; [MO] = 5 mg L−1), (B) and (D) Plots of ln (C 0/C) vs irradiation time.

(A) The open circuit photo-voltage decay (OCPD) measurements (comparisons of photoelectron lifetime and OCP in dark and light (inset)), (B) Steady-state PL spectra, and (C) Time-resolved PL spectra of {001}TiO2/Ti and CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti photoelectrodes, (D) The EPR spectra of {001}TiO2 and CQDs-{001}TiO2 samples under the visible light.
The photocurrent density, impendence and carrier density (N D) of four CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti photoelectrodes with different CQDs contents.

head.ce:title: 3D CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti photoelectrode with dominant {001} facets: Efficient visible-light-driven photoelectrocatalytic oxidation of organic pollutants and mechanism insight

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Science and Engineering, Shanghai Key Lab of Chemical Assessment and Sustainability, Key Laboratory of Yangtze River Water Environment, Tongji University, Shanghai 200092, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Science and Engineering
Shanghai Key Lab of Chemical Assessment and Sustainability
Key Laboratory of Yangtze River Water Environment

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Tongji University, 1239 Siping Road, Shanghai, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A three dimensional (3D) carbon quantum dots (CQDs) decorated {001}TiO2/Ti photoelectrode was prepared by constructing the CQDs on the surface of {001} TiO2, which were grown in-situ on Ti-mesh structure, providing the nearly 100% exposed active {001} facets. The 3D CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti shows high PEC oxidation activity towards the MO and BPA under visible light radiation, while the relevant removal ratios are 94.8% for BPA in 1 h and 93.6% for MO in 2 h, respectively. The enhanced PEC activity of CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti is mainly attributed to the three improved factors (η abs, η sep and η inj), which is analyzed deeply. The existence of CQDs not only improve the η abs since its superior up-conversion performance, but also promote the higher η sep due to enhanced hole-electron pairs’ separation. The highly exposed {001} facets are favorable for oxidation reactions to provide the increased η inj.

ce:section.ce:para: The rapid population growth and global industrialization result in set free various toxic compounds, including endocrine disrupting compounds (EDCs), dye, heavy metals, pharmaceuticals and personal care products (PPCPs), etc. These pollutants released into water have caused numerous threats to human health and environment, which become a major focus of scientific attention. Therefore, various techniques have been employed for water purification, such as adsorption, biodegradation, catalytic oxidation, photocatalytic and photoelectrocatalytic (PEC) [1–5]. Compared with other methods with some shortcomings in practical applications, such as complex processing, high time cost, and low mineralization efficiency, PEC technique has been recognized as a highly efficient way for treating wastewater since it integrates the advantages of both photocatalytic and the electrochemical method [6,7].

PEC reactions are essentially interfacial reactions [8], pollutants are adsorbed on the PEC interface by diffusion and then the catalytic oxidation reaction occurs immediately. The PEC efficiency is mainly determined by three factors, namely, light absorption efficiency (η abs), charge separation efficiency (η sep) and interfacial charge injection (η inj) [9,10]. Therefore, in order to obtain an idea PEC interface, constructing an efficient photoelectrode material with high η abs, η sep and η inj would be an effective way.

Among a variety of semiconductors for PEC reaction, titanium dioxide (TiO2), a representative photocatalyst, has received much attention for its non-toxicity, optics, low cost and biocompatibility [6,11]. Recently, many studies have indicated that the TiO2 materials with tailored nanocrystal facets can greatly improve the surface activity in PEC reactions. To date, the anatase TiO2 with different {001} exposure ratios have been widely reported for the reason of its higher surface energy (0.90 J/m2) and 100% unsaturated Ti5c atoms [12–14]. However, {001}TiO2 materials still suffer the shortcoming of a wide bandgap (3.2 eV) [15], which result in its limited utilization under solar light. Introducing the narrow band gap semiconductors, noble metals, or dyes to {001}TiO2 by doping, sensitization, or deposition become the efficient way to improve its PEC properties [16,17]. Among them, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as a novel luminescent material attracts a great deal of attention for PEC system, owing to its unique up-conversion optical properties and charge transfer capabilities [18–24].

In this work, we propose a novel 3D CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti photoelectrode through decorating the CQDs on the TiO2 microspheres “mirror like” surface via sample hydrothermal method, while the {001}TiO2 microspheres with nearly 100% exposed {001} facets are grown in situ on Ti-mesh structure. Firstly, the introduction of CQDs provides the improved η abs, since it can achieve the conversion from visible radiation to ultraviolet-visible light due to its up-conversion performance, thereby, the {001}TiO2 can be excited. Secondly, the CQDs are in favor of the separation between the photoexcited holes and electrons, implying the higher η sep. Thirdly, the highly exposed {001} facets of CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti are attractive for holes, which is favorable for oxidation reactions and promotes the η inj. Furthermore, 3D CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti photoelectrode based on Ti mesh substrate structure could be folded and tailored in size optionally, which present a good prospect of practical application. The PEC applications of the as-prepared photoelectrode are evaluated by the removal of both bisphenol A (BPA) and methylene orange (MO), which are typical EDCs and dye pollutants. BPA is widely used as plasticizers, which could easily enter to the human’s digestive system and respiratory system through food, drinking water, etc., thus affecting the human’s endocrine system. Meanwhile, MO have wide industrial applications, but cause serious environmental issues due to its high toxicity and accumulation in the environment [25–27]. Due to their widespread existence and huge potential threat in environment, the removal of BPA and MO have received great attention. Finally, the possible PEC mechanism is proposed based on the experimental results.

Ti mesh were first polished with chemical polish fluid (VHF:VHNO3:VH2O = 1:5:50) for 20 s and ultrasonic cleaned in distilled water and ethyl alcohol for 10 min. After drying, adding it into a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave with a mixture of 27 μL 38 wt% HF and 30 mL deionized water, and then heated at 180 °C for 4 h. After that, the Ti mesh was taken out and cleaned with deionized water, then the Ti mesh was annealed at 450 °C for 3 h in an air atmosphere after drying to obtain {001}TiO2/Ti.

The CQDs solution was prepared by Wan’s reported work [28]. The as-prepared CQDs solution was dialyzed for 3 h to remove the large particles and impurities. The brown CQDs solution with the concentration of 2.0 mg/mL was obtained and stored at 4 °C before using. Dilute the CQDs solution obtained above to 0.5, 1.0, 2.0 mg/mL, and took 30 mL solution into 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave, respectively. Then, the as-prepared {001}TiO2/Ti was added into it and kept at 100 °C for 2 h. After that, washed electrodes with deionized water and dried. The 3D CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti were prepared and marked with 0.5, 1.0, 2.0 CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti.

The micromorphology of photoelectrodes was investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100,) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi–S4800). The Bruker D8 Focus Advance X-ray diffractometer (XRD) was used to determine the structure and crystal phase of materials. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra and UV–vis diffuse reflection spectroscopy (DRS) were conducted on F-7000 with excitation of 320 nm and Agilent Carry5000. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Kratos-ASIS-HS) was used to analysis the elements and atomic valence state of samples. Raman spectra (Renishaw Crop.) and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR, Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS10) spectrums were carried out for characterize the functional groups. Electron paramagnetic resonance signals (EPR, Bruker, EMX plus-10/12) were used to detect the active species. The time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopy measurement was conducted on a HORIBA Fluorolog-3-11 fluorescence spectrometer.

All PEC tests were measured in a typical three-electrode cell system, which consists of the CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti or {001}TiO2/Ti as the working electrode, platinum foil as a counter electrode and the saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as a reference. Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV), current-time (i–t) curves, Mott-Schottky plots and Nyquist plots of the as-prepared photoanode materials were tested in 0.1 M Na2SO4 electrolyte solution under light irradiation by a 150 W LA-410UV-3 lamp (Hayashi, Japan). A 420-nm cutoff filter and an AM 1.5 filter were used for visible-light irradiation and simulated sunlight, respectively. The distance between the light source and the working electrode was 1 cm.

The quartz cube with an effective volume of 40 mL is used as a reaction tank for degradation of target contaminants. The 40 mL BPA of 1 mg/L or MO of 5 mg/L is degraded with 0.1 mol L−1 Na2SO4 solution as the supporting electrolyte. The PEC degradation was carried at positive potential (+0.4 V vs. SCE) under visible light irradiation (light source: PLS-SXE300, Perfect Light Technology Co. Ltd., Beijing, China, with 420 nm optical filter). The concentration of BPA and MO was analyzed by high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC, Agilent 1260, running conditions: methanol/water (V/V = 65:35 for BPA, V/V = 38:62 for MO) used as mobile phase, 1 mL/min flow rate, C18 column, wavelength of 225 nm for BPA and 464 nm for MO).

For EPR measurements, the aqueous solutions of samples were mixed with 5, 5-dimethypylpyrroline-1-oxide (DMPO) of 50 μL. And the signal of DMPO-
OH adducts were created under both visible and UV–vis light irradiation.

Fig. 1 shows representative SEM images of the 3D CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti electrode. Fig. 1A shows the unique network-like structure of Ti-mesh substrate. Unlike on planar Ti foil where TiO2 microspheres are grown on 2D substrate, the TiO2 microspheres with the diameter of 0.8–1.5 μm grown in-situ on Ti mesh are radially extended in 3D structure. The Fig. 1B shows that the surface of TiO2 microspheres look like a “mirror”, which are composed of very closely aligned square crystal facets. This is further supported by the TEM result in Fig. 1C. The as-prepared CQDs with the diameter of 3–5 nm are shown in Fig.1D, and the HRTEM image (insert image) displays the lattices with fringe spacing of 0.218 nm corresponding to the {100} planes of graphitic carbon [28,29]. Fig. 1E and F show the TEM images of {001}TiO2/Ti before and after loading the CQDs. The stripe spacing between the two lattice diffraction fringes are measured as 0.351 nm (Fig. 1E), corresponding to the {101} crystal plane, inferring the side of the truncated square prism is {101} crystal plane. The angle between the top and the side is about 111.7°, which indicates that the top square crystal facets is {001} crystal plane according to the growth model of the anatase crystal [30]. The percentage of exposed {001} facets was calculated as nearly 95% [12], providing the high exposure of active facets. Compared with pure {001}TiO2, lots of CQDs are dispersed uniformly on the surface of {001}TiO2. And the loading of CQDs does not completely cover the {001} facets since its nanosize, which largely ensure the high exposure of {001} facets and significantly influence the interfacial reaction efficiency (ηox) of the catalyst. These results confirmed the successful construction of 3D CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti photoelectrode.

Fig. S1A illustrates the XRD patterns of {001}TiO2/Ti, CQDs and CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti. The distinct diffraction peaks are mainly located at 62.7°, 55.1°, 53.9°, 48°, 37.8° and 25.3°, which attributed to the (204), (211), (105), (200), (004) and (101) crystal facets of anatase TiO2 (JCPDS, Card NO. 21-1272) [13], respectively. Otherwise, the relatively weak diffraction peaks at 41.2°, 36.1°and 27.4° are observed respectively, which correspond to the (111), (101) and (110) crystal planes of rutile TiO2 (JCPDS, Card NO. 21-1276) [31]. It indicates the existence of a small amount of rutile TiO2. The sharp (004) diffraction peaks of TiO2 confirm the exposed {001} facets of as-prepared TiO2. A broad peak between 15° and 30° are observed on pure CQDs, which related to the (002) diffraction plane of graphite [19]. The XRD pattern of CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti is basically the same as that of {001}TiO2, verifying that the existence of CQDs does not change the crystal phase of TiO2. The FT-IR spectra in Fig.S1B characterize the functional groups of CQDs-{001}TiO2. For CQDs, the adsorption peaks at 1434, 1567 and 2987 cm−1 associated with the vibration of COH, CO and sp3 CH, respectively. In the case of CQDs-{001}TiO2, the typical characteristic peaks of CQDs are also found, which reveal the existence of CQDs in CQDs-{001}TiO2 [31]. The absorption peak in 1631 cm−1 could be attributed to the bending vibration peak of OH bond of surface absorbed water. The hydroxyl (TiO2 OH) could react with COH and OC of CQDs and form TiOC bonds at the surface of TiO2. The broad adsorption band (<1000 cm−1) has a shift in CQDs-{001}TiO2 with respect to {001}TiO2, indicating the combination of TiOTi and TiOC vibrations [32].

The Raman spectrums of three samples are shown in Fig. S1C and S1D. Five strong vibration peaks appear in both {001}TiO2/Ti and CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti, which are attributed to the typical characteristics of anatase TiO2. Otherwise, the two distinct peaks located at 1348 and 1575 cm−1 for CQDs are corresponding to the sp3 carbon (D-band) and sp2 carbon (G-band) of carbon, respectively. A slight shift of G-band towards lower wavenumber (1569 cm−1) is observed on CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti. It is mainly ascribed to the chemical doping of carbonaceous materials, which indicates weak chemical bonding or electron interaction between CQDs and the {001}TiO2 [33]. Furthermore, the ID/IG of CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti is higher than that of CQDs, owing to the increase of lattice disorders and defects of CQDs in composites. The XPS measurement was further used to demonstrate the interaction between CQDs and {001} TiO2 (Fig. S2). The full survey spectrum (Fig. S2A) confirms the presence of O, Ti and C in the CQDs-{001}TiO2. The deconvoluted O 1s XPS spectrum (Fig.S 2B) presents three peaks at 533.2, 530.9 and 529.5 eV are corresponding to the OH, CO, and TiO bonds, respectively. The Ti 2p1/2 and Ti 2p2/3 peaks of CQDs-{001} TiO2 are centered at about 464.1 eV and 458.3 eV (Fig. S2C). The binding energies of CO and Ti 2p2/3 have slightly shifted with respect to {001}TiO2, indicating the forceful interaction between {001}TiO2 and CQDs [31]. In the C 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. S2D), the deconvoluted peaks at 287.5, 286.0 and 284.6 eV are assigned to the CO, CO and CC (graphite) bond of CQDs, which could also be found in CQDs-{001} TiO2. These results confirm that the CQDs were successfully combined with {001}TiO2. No peak of TiC bond is found in both C 1s and Ti 2p spectra, which reveals that the CQDs does not exist as a dopant in {001}TiO2 [33]. These evidences further supported the FT-IR and Raman spectroscopy results.

Fig. 2 A illustrates the UV–vis diffuse reflection (DRS) analysis of {001}TiO2/Ti and CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti. The pure {001}TiO2/Ti exhibits the absorption band edge of 389 nm and the corresponding bandgap is calculated as 3.15 eV (Fig. 2A insert). For comparison, the CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti provides an obviously enhanced spectrum ranging from 390 nm to 610 nm with the narrower bandgap of 3.05 eV (Fig. 2A insert). The results indicate that the loading of CQDs can efficiently expand the light absorption range into visible light region. The fluorescence emission spectrum of CQDs was measured in Fig. 2B. As shown, the CQDs exhibits the emission wavelength range from 350 nm to 410 nm (shorter wavelength) under the excitation wavelength of 700–900 nm (longer wavelength), which confirms the excellent up-conversion characteristics of CQDs. Therefore, the {001}TiO2 can be excited in visible light since the CQDs can convert longer excitation wavelength to shorter emission wavelength.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PEC performance of 3D CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti under visible light irradiation
ce:section.ce:para:
To optimize the PEC properties of the photoelectrodes, three kinds of 3D CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti samples were prepared by adjusting the concentration of the CQDs solution. The PEC properties were studied in Fig. 3 . As shown in Fig. 3A, the photocurrent response of {001}TiO2/Ti electrode is not sensitive under the visible light illumination. In contrast, all of the 3D CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti photoelectrodes show enhanced photocurrent responses. Compared with 0.5 CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti and 2.0 CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti, 1.0 CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti provides the maximum photocurrent at the voltage of 0–1.2 V and the optimal photocurrent response is obtained at the voltage of 0.4 V. Fig. 3B displays the photocurrent-time (i–t) curves of samples under the 0.4 V. The photocurrents of all photoelectrodes are reproducible and stable and the relevant values follow the order of 1.0 CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti > 2.0 CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti > 0.5 CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti > {001}TiO2/Ti, which is consistent with the LSV results. The photocurrent density obtained on 1.0 CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti is 0.343 mA/cm−2, which is 1.62 and 1.35 times of 0.5 CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti (0.212 mA/cm−2) and 2.0 CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti (0.255 mA/cm−2) respectively. Fig. 3C illustrates the EIS Nyquist plots of photoelectrodes and the results show that the {001}TiO2/Ti displays the biggest arc radius, which indicates the highest charge transfer resistance. Compared with 0.5 CQDs-{001} TiO2/Ti and 2.0 CQDs-{001} TiO2/Ti, the 1.0 CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti exhibit the smallest arc radius, which signifies the most rapid charge transfer capability and lowest carriers recombination rate. The Mott-Schottky plots are shown in Fig. 3D. All of the plots exhibit a positive slope, indicating their n-type semiconductor characteristic as the majority carriers [34,35]. Furthermore, the donor density (ND ) are calculated through the Eq. (1) [36] to describe the energy level model for the charge transfer and the relevant results are listed in Table 1 .
N D
=
2
e ε
ε 0
d E
d
1
C
S C
2
1/Csc
2 = 2 × (ΔΦsc – RT/F)/(eε0εND )

In contrast to pure {001}TiO2, all of the CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti photoelectrodes provided the improved donor density. And the 1.0 CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti has the highest ND of 4.869 × 1021 cm−3, which is about 3.50 and 9.11 times that of 0.5 CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti (1.390 × 1021 cm−3) and 2.0 CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti (5.345 × 1020 cm−3), respectively. All of the above results indicate that the introduction of CQDs can greatly improve the PEC activity of {001}TiO2/Ti, which can be maximally optimized through controlling the content of CQDs. Among the samples with different CQDs content, the 1.0 CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti shows the best PEC performance. Besides, the comparison of PEC performance of {001}TiO2/Ti and 1.0 CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti were also carried under the simulated sunlight (Fig. S3). The 1.0 CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti provides the higher photocurrent (0.84 mA/cm2) and smaller charge transfer resistance (175 Ω) than that of pure {001}TiO2/Ti (0.30 mA/cm2 and 350 Ω). The results demonstrate the enhanced PEC performance of 3D CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti are not only obtained in the visible light region but also can be maintained in the solar light region.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PEC degradation and oxidation kinetic of BPA and MO
ce:section.ce:para: The PEC oxidation abilities of pollutants under visible light irradiation are further investigated by applying the 1.0 CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti (referred as CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti) as the photoelectrode. Firstly, the BPA solution of 1 mg/L was chosen for the target sample since it has reported that the concentration of BPA in effluent of wastewater plant and landfill leachate are from 0.37 mg L−1 to 17.2 mg L−1 [37]. The removal efficiencies of BPA under different conditions are studied in detail (Fig. 4 A). In a single electrocatalysis (EC) and photocatalysis (PC) oxidation process, the removal rate of BPA obtained on {001}TiO2/Ti are 4.1% and 14.4% in 1 h, respectively. And its PEC removal rate of BPA is only 34.0% with the combined effect of EC and PC oxidations. However, in the case of CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti, the PEC removal rate is improved greatly and can be reached to 94.8% in 1 h, indicating the enhanced synergistic effects of EC and PC. The PEC degradation kinetics of BPA are shown in Fig. 4B. The ln (C 0/C) vs the irradiation time during all process show a good linear relationship, which are according with the pseudo-first-order kinetics plot. The value of apparent rate constant (k) of CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti is 0.051 min−1, which is about 6.8 times higher than that of {001}TiO2/Ti (0.007 min−1).

Otherwise, as one kind of typical dye pollutants, MO was chosen as the second target contaminants. Since the MO is easy to accumulate in wastewater, a lot of research focused on the removal of MO with concentration of 2–10 mg L−1 [38,39]. Here, the MO solution of 5 mg/L was chosen for the target sample. It can be seen in Fig. 4C that the single EC and PC oxidation process on {001}TiO2/Ti promote the removal rate of 12.9% and 7.8% within 2 h respectively, while the PEC oxidation removal ratio of MO is about 21.1%. In contrast, the CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti provides the high PEC removal ratio of 93.6%, and the relevant k value is 0.024 min−1 which is almost improved 10 times in comparison to {001}TiO2/Ti (0.002 min−1). The above results strongly demonstrated that the CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti has great potentiality in PEC removal of pollutants in visible light, which is mainly ascribed to the introduction of CQDs that not only achieve the utilization of visible light but also enhance the PEC synergy effect.·

The cycling test results of the PEC activity of photoelectrodes for degrading BPA and MO are shown in Fig. S4. After 5 cycles, the degradation rates of BPA and MO on {001}TiO2/Ti decrease slightly to 30.5% and 20.6%, respectively. Otherwise, the CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti still achieve the effective degradation of BPA and MO, while the rates are remained at 94.2% and 90.0% respectively even after 5 cycles. The results indicate the CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti can work as the effective and stable photoelectrode for degradation of organic compounds.

In order to deeply find out the possible reasons for the high PEC oxidation ability of the CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti electrode, the synergy effect between CQDs and {001}TiO2/Ti was further investigated in detail.

Actually, there are three parameters related to the PEC efficiency, they are the light absorption efficiency (η abs), charge separation efficiency (η sep) and interfacial charge injection (η inj), respectively [9,39]. It is clearly shown in Fig. 2A that the modification of CQDs greatly expanded the light absorption range of photoelectrode, which provided the improved η abs [40,41].

The η sep is determined by the intensity of band bending related to the Fermi level pinning effect and electron transport capability which relies on the conductivity of the photoelectrode [42,43]. Open circuit potential decay (OCPD) technique is conducted to study the band bending intensity of photoelectrodes (Fig. 5 A). The insert image presents the difference between OCP in case of the dark and light (ΔOCP), which was measured as 0.035 V for bare {001}TiO2/Ti and 0.13 V for CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti. The larger ΔOCP of latter demonstrates the more intense band bending, which is ascribed to its greater extent Fermi level pinning effect originated form the special surface states [43,44]. Otherwise, the lifetime of photoelectron (τ) was also calculated by Eq. (2) [45] to evaluate the recombination of photoexcited e−– h+ pairs.
(2)
τ = (k B T/e)(dV oc/dt)−1

Fig. 5A shows the calculated τ in line with V oc through monitoring photovoltage decay (V oc) over time by turning off illumination at steady state. The results reveal that the CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti has the much longer electron lifetime in comparison to bare {001}TiO2, implying the lower recombination of photoexcited pairs. The Nyquist plots in Fig. 3D also have confirmed the better conductivity of CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti with respect to {001}TiO2/Ti. Moreover, the η sep of bare {001}TiO2/Ti and CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti by means of PL spectroscopy is discussed in detail. Fig. 5B shows the steady-state PL spectra for photoelectrodes, the two typical PL emission peaks center at 398 nm and 468 nm respectively. It shows that the loading of CQDs result in the decreased PL intensity, which reveals the higher photogenerated charges separation that derived from the unique photogenerated electron transfer characteristics of CQDs. In addition, the time-resolved fluorescence measurement (excitation at 290 nm and emission at 468 nm) was conducted in Fig. 5C. The CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti shows the shorter PL lifetime (cf. 8.22 ns) by a factor of 1.18 in comparison to {001}TiO2/Ti (cf. 9.68 ns). It is mainly attributed to the photoinduced electron transfer feature of CQDs, which can efficiently trap photogenerated electrons from {001}TiO2 and provides an enhanced separation efficiency [46]. Both the PL quenching and time-resolved PL spectra confirm that the introduction of CQDs can effectively suppress the recombination of photoexcited hole-electron pairs and greatly promote the η sep [47].

The η inj indicates the yield of those holes reached on the photoelectrode interface and injected into the electrolyte [39,48]. The highly exposed {001} facets constructed in CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti are attractive for holes since its higher surface energy (0.90 J/m2) and favorable for oxidation reactions, which could provide the higher η inj [49]. The EPR spin-trap technique was utilized to investigate the main active species under the visible light irradiation (Fig. 5D) [50,51], since the amount of
OH radicals is determined by the total number of holes. The characteristic signals of DMPO-
OH adducts are observed in both {001}TiO2/Ti and CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti, indicating the generation of
OH radicals. It can be noted that the peak intensity of
OH over CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti is much higher than that of bare {001}TiO2/Ti, verifying its higher catalytic ability. This conclusion were further supported by the EPR test under the UV–vis light irradiation (Fig S5).

Based on the above studies, the possible PEC oxidation mechanism of pollutants over CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti electrode is proposed in Scheme 1 . Firstly, CQDs can absorb the visible light and convert it into ultraviolet light, thereby the {001}TiO2 is excited successfully and generate high concentration of photoexcited carries. Secondly, in this process, photo-excited carriers could be separated on the interface between the photoelectrode and electrolyte in the space charge layer, owing to the band bending force. But the majority of the excited electrons and holes generated outside the space charge layer will recombine [42]. Here, the CQDs serve as the electron reservoirs to trap the photoinduced electrons from {001}TiO2, which efficiently accelerate the separation of holes and electrons [33,52]. Meanwhile, the residual photoelectrons on {001}TiO2 can transfer to the Ti mesh quickly and finally transfer to the cathode under the driving force of an electric field. After that, the migrated photogenerated holes on surface of {001}TiO2 can react with H2O to produce the
OH, which provide the high oxidation capability of the pollutants. Finally, an enhanced PEC activity of CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti can be obtained based on the great synergy effect not only between CQDs and {001}TiO2/Ti, but also between the PC and EC oxidation process.

In summary, a 3D CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti electrode has been first successfully prepared through constructing the CQDs on the {001}TiO2/Ti mesh surface with 3D structure and nearly 100% exposed {001}facets. The CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti electrode can efficiently remove MO and BPA wastewater under visible light radiation, and the relevant degradation removal ratios are 94.8% for 1 mg/L BPA in 1 h and 93.6% for 5 mg/L MO in 2 h respectively. The enhanced PEC activity of CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti is mainly attributed to the three improved factors (η abs, η sep and η inj), which have close relationship with the PEC efficiency. The η abs is improved by the superior up-conversion performance of CQDs. The η sep is promoted since the existence of CQDs benefits the hole-electron pairs’ separation. The highly exposed {001} facets are favorable for oxidation reactions and enhence the η inj. Furthermore, the 3D CQDs-{001}TiO2/Ti photoelectrode based on Ti mesh substrate structure could be folded and tailored in size optionally, which has great potential in environmental practical application. This study provide valuable information and new insights to design the efficient PEC photoelectrode for environmental pollution treatment.
The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundations of China (NSFC, No. 21876133, 21876128, 21537003), Science & Technology Commission of Shanghai Municipality (14DZ2261100), the State Key Laboratory of Pollution Control and Resource Reuse Foundation, (No. PCRRF18013), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apcatb.2019.118229.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
